knowledge_comprehension:
(a) What is soil erosion?

Soil erosion is the process of detachment and transportation of topsoil particles from one place to another by natural agencies, primarily water and wind.

(b) Explain the practice of planting 3–4 seedlings of 30–40 days old per hill.

Planting 3–4 seedlings of 30–40 days old per hill represents the traditional or conventional method of transplanted Boro rice cultivation widely used by farmers.


In this approach, older seedlings are used to withstand harsh outdoor field conditions and water level variations. Multiple seedlings are planted together in a single hill as an insurance measure, ensuring that even if one or two seedlings die due to transplanting shock or pests, the remaining ones can survive to secure the intended plant population across the field.
full_cq:
(a) What is soil erosion?

Soil erosion is the process of detachment and transportation of topsoil particles from one place to another by natural agencies, primarily water and wind.

(b) Explain the practice of planting 3–4 seedlings of 30–40 days old per hill.

Planting 3–4 seedlings of 30–40 days old per hill represents the traditional or conventional method of transplanted Boro rice cultivation widely used by farmers.


In this approach, older seedlings are used to withstand harsh outdoor field conditions and water level variations. Multiple seedlings are planted together in a single hill as an insurance measure, ensuring that even if one or two seedlings die due to transplanting shock or pests, the remaining ones can survive to secure the intended plant population across the field.

(c) Explain the method 'A' mentioned in the stem.

Method 'A' mentioned in the stem, which involves planting a single young seedling per hill, represents the System of Rice Intensification (SRI).


In this scientific management technique, exceptionally young seedlings—typically 8 to 12 days old with only two leaves—are carefully uprooted from the nursery along with their seed-sac and soil matrix to prevent any root trauma. A single seedling is then transplanted gently per hill at a wide, square spacing of $25\text{ cm} \times 25\text{ cm}$ or $30\text{ cm} \times 30\text{ cm}$.


Instead of keeping the rice field continuously flooded with water, this method utilizes an Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD) irrigation pattern. This intermittent watering provides the roots with both moisture and abundant oxygen, preventing root degradation and encouraging the development of a massive, healthy root architecture capable of absorbing optimal nutrients.

(d) Analyze which method between 'A' and 'B' mentioned in the stem is more profitable in rice cultivation.

Comparison of Seed Requirements and Input Costs: Method 'A' (SRI) is vastly more profitable than Method 'B' (Conventional) primarily because it slashes input expenditures right from the start. Since Method 'A' requires only one seedling per hill at a wider spacing, it reduces the seed requirement by up to 80% (needing only 2–3 kg of seed per acre compared to 15–20 kg in Method 'B'), saving considerable capital for marginalized farmers.


Tiller Production and Growth Dynamics: In Method 'B', the older 30–40 day seedlings experience severe transplanting shock, and their dense grouping creates intense competition for sunlight and nutrients, limiting output to 10–12 tillers per hill. Conversely, the single young seedling in Method 'A' adapts instantly to the soil and utilizes the ample surrounding space and sunlight to produce 30–50 robust, healthy tillers per hill, resulting in much larger panicles and higher grain counts.


Water and Pest Management Efficiencies: Method 'B' relies on continuous flooding, which consumes immense irrigation water, drives up diesel/electricity bills, and fosters a humid environment ideal for destructive pests. Method 'A' uses Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD), saving up to 40%–50% of precious irrigation water while reducing pest and disease outbreaks, which eliminates the need for expensive chemical pesticide applications.


Conclusion on Net Profitability: In conclusion, Method 'A' emerges as the highly profitable option for modern Boro rice cultivation. By reducing input costs (seeds, water, labor, and pesticides) while boosting overall grain yield by 20% to 30%, it delivers a significantly higher net financial return per acre for farmers compared to the traditional practices of Method 'B'.

fullCQData.stem: Chand Miah is a farmer. Every year, he makes a profit by cultivating rice, chili, and potato. But last year, due to the rising water levels of the Jamuna River, a vast area was submerged. After the water receded, instead of fertile silt soil, all his land was covered with sand. He is now sitting with his hands on his head, shedding tears. Seeing this, the Sub-Assistant Agriculture Officer advised him to add organic matter and take a few other measures to improve the quality of the soil. Working accordingly, Chand Miah successfully cultivated crops on his land again and became profitable. Following his example, other farmers were also benefited.
knowledge_comprehension:
(a) What is irrigation?

Irrigation is the artificial application of water to the soil or land for assisting in the growth and production of agricultural crops.

(b) What is meant by green manure?

Green manure refers to the practice of growing specific, quick-growing crops—predominantly leguminous varieties like sunn hemp, dhaincha, or cowpea—and plowing them back directly into the soil while still green.


This agricultural practice is carried out to enrich the soil with vital organic matter, fix atmospheric nitrogen through root nodules, and significantly enhance the overall physical, chemical, and biological health of the topsoil without causing environmental pollution.
full_cq:
(a) What is irrigation?

Irrigation is the artificial application of water to the soil or land for assisting in the growth and production of agricultural crops.

(b) What is meant by green manure?

Green manure refers to the practice of growing specific, quick-growing crops—predominantly leguminous varieties like sunn hemp, dhaincha, or cowpea—and plowing them back directly into the soil while still green.


This agricultural practice is carried out to enrich the soil with vital organic matter, fix atmospheric nitrogen through root nodules, and significantly enhance the overall physical, chemical, and biological health of the topsoil without causing environmental pollution.

(c) Explain how Chand Miah became profitable by cultivating crops on his land.

Chand Miah became profitable by aggressively modifying the sandy deposits on his land through the heavy application of organic matter and chemical amendments as advised by the expert.


The massive flood from the Jamuna River left thick layers of infertile sand over his arable plots. Sandy soil possesses extremely large macro-pores, which cause rapid water drainage, high percolation losses, and a total lack of vital plant nutrients. To counter this, Chand Miah mixed compost, cow dung, and green manure into the sand, which effectively bound the loose sand particles together into structured soil aggregates.


As a direct result of these structural enhancements, the water-holding capacity and nutrient retention ability of his land were restored. This allowed his crops—rice, chili, and potato—to safely absorb water and essential minerals throughout their growth cycles, turning his barren, sandy fields back into highly productive zones that yielded rich, marketable harvests.

(d) Evaluate the justification of Chand Miah's activities in light of the agricultural officer's advice.

Context of Soil Degradation: The advice given by the Sub-Assistant Agriculture Officer was completely justified and scientifically accurate because river-induced sand deposition (sanding) destroys the active root-zone environment of standard crops. Left untreated, pure sand cannot support moisture-loving crops like rice or root-expanding crops like potatoes due to nutrient starvation and excessive drainage, which initially caused Chand Miah's severe distress.


Scientific Impact of Organic Matter: The core of the officer's recommendation was the heavy addition of organic matter, which serves as a panacea for sandy tracts. When organic material decomposes into humus, it acts like a sponge, dramatically reducing the high infiltration rate of sandy layers, increasing micro-pore space, and lowering soil temperature. It also provides essential primary nutrients ($N, P, K$) and fosters a thriving population of beneficial soil microbes.


Adoption of Complementary Practices: In addition to adding organic matter, Chand Miah followed the advice to take "a few other measures." These must have included mixing heavy clay soil from external sources to permanently balance the texture, utilizing balanced chemical dosing, and adopting frequent, light irrigation schedules suited for high-permeability soils. This comprehensive management plan transformed the sandy desert-like deposit into a highly fertile, loam-behaving matrix.


Conclusion and Socio-Economic Validation: In conclusion, Chand Miah's immediate actions based on scientific advice were highly justified and profitable. Not only did these interventions rescue his livelihood from total ruin and bring financial prosperity, but they also established a successful, localized demonstration plot. This motivated neighboring flood-affected farmers to adopt identical soil rehabilitation technologies, generating an expansive economic recovery across the entire community.

The image features a pipe structure with nozzle heads pressurized to spray water upwards and outwards over a crop field, labeled as "Figure: Water Irrigation Method."
knowledge_comprehension:
(a) What is biofertilizer?

Biofertilizer is a substance containing living or latent cells of beneficial microorganisms that increase the nutrient availability and fertility of the soil when applied to seeds, plant surfaces, or soil.

(b) What is meant by neutral soil?

Neutral soil refers to soil that is neither acidic nor alkaline, possessing a hydrogen ion concentration profile that translates exactly to a pH value of 7.0 (or a narrow optimal range between 6.5 and 7.3).


In neutral soil, almost all essential plant nutrients—such as nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium, calcium, and magnesium—remain highly soluble and readily available for root absorption. It also provides the most ideal environment for the growth and activity of beneficial soil microorganisms like nitrogen-fixing bacteria and earthworms.
full_cq:
(a) What is biofertilizer?

Biofertilizer is a substance containing living or latent cells of beneficial microorganisms that increase the nutrient availability and fertility of the soil when applied to seeds, plant surfaces, or soil.

(b) What is meant by neutral soil?

Neutral soil refers to soil that is neither acidic nor alkaline, possessing a hydrogen ion concentration profile that translates exactly to a pH value of 7.0 (or a narrow optimal range between 6.5 and 7.3).


In neutral soil, almost all essential plant nutrients—such as nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium, calcium, and magnesium—remain highly soluble and readily available for root absorption. It also provides the most ideal environment for the growth and activity of beneficial soil microorganisms like nitrogen-fixing bacteria and earthworms.

(c) Which water irrigation method described in your textbook is similar to the method shown in the figure? Explain.

The water irrigation method shown in the figure is identical to the Sprinkler Irrigation method described in the textbook.


In this method, water is led under high pressure through a network of pipes and sprayed into the air through rotating or stationary nozzle heads, breaking up into small droplets before falling onto the crop canopy and ground. The entire process closely mimics natural rainfall, ensuring uniform moisture delivery across the soil surface without creating high-velocity runoff.


This system is highly mechanical and consists of a pumping unit, mainlines, sub-mains, and lateral lines equipped with perforated sprinkler heads. It is specifically designed to distribute water evenly across fields where traditional surface flooding techniques are physically impractical or highly inefficient.

(d) Analyze the logical justification of irrigating water using the method mentioned in the stem across all types of fields.

Suitability for Topographical Terrain: Sprinkler irrigation is highly justified for undulating, uneven, or hilly terrains where land leveling is too expensive or physically impossible. Traditional surface irrigation causes massive water loss and soil erosion on slopes, whereas sprinklers apply water softly from above, keeping the soil intact regardless of the ground slope.


Efficiency in Light-Textured Sandy Soils: In sandy fields with exceptionally high infiltration rates, surface flooding leads to immediate deep percolation losses before the water can spread across the plot. Sprinkler irrigation is the most logical choice here because it delivers water at a controlled application rate that matches the soil absorption capacity, preventing wastage and conserving groundwater resources.


Water Conservation and Crop Protection: This method achieves a high water application efficiency of up to 75%–80% by eliminating seepage and evaporation losses common in open earthen channels. Furthermore, the fine mist created by sprinklers protects sensitive crops from extreme microclimate fluctuations, washes dust off leaf surfaces to enhance photosynthesis, and can prevent frost damage during winter nights.


Limitations and Comprehensive Evaluation: However, the statement "justified across all fields" must be qualified because sprinkler irrigation is not practical for every scenario. It is highly unsuitable for tall-statured crops (like sugarcane) that block the spray path, hot and windy regions where wind drifts distort the spray uniformity, and water-intensive lowland crops like Boro rice that require continuous ponding. Therefore, while it is technically superior for high-value crops, sandy tracts, and undulating terrain, its high installation cost and physical limitations mean it is not universally optimal for all agricultural setups.

fullCQData.stem: The farmers of Sujalpur village have been cultivating rice using conventional methods for a long time. Inspired by this, some farmers cultivated rice using this method and became highly profitable. Following them, other farmers were also benefited.
knowledge_comprehension:
(a) What is Trichoderma?

Trichoderma is a genus of free-living, soil-borne fungi that is widely used as an eco-friendly biocontrol agent to suppress plant pathogenic fungi and stimulate plant growth.

(b) Why is mulching done in crop fields?

Mulching is done in crop fields primarily to conserve soil moisture by creating a physical barrier over the soil surface that restricts evaporation losses caused by solar radiation.


Additionally, a well-applied mulch blocks sunlight from reaching the soil surface, which effectively suppresses weed germination and growth. It also helps regulate soil temperature, prevents topsoil erosion from heavy rain impacts, and enriches soil organic matter as biodegradable mulch materials decompose over time.

(b) Why is mulching done in crop fields?

(c) Explain why rice cultivation using the new method mentioned in the stem is profitable.

Rice cultivation using the new method—the System of Rice Intensification (SRI)—is highly profitable because it vastly optimizes production dynamics while sharply cutting down the farmers' overhead expenses.


The method dictates transplanting only a single young seedling (8–12 days old) per hill with wide spacing instead of bunching multiple older seedlings tightly together. This wide spatial framework eliminates aggressive inter-plant competition for nutrition, allowing each individual plant to capture maximum sunlight and airflow, which naturally stimulates the development of a massive, robust root system.


Consequently, this enhanced root architecture drives the production of a remarkably higher number of effective, grain-bearing tillers per hill (often 30–40 tillers). The increased tiller volume paired with a substantial reduction in seed costs, lower chemical fertilizer inputs, and optimized water management exponentially maximizes the net crop yield and financial profit margins for the farmers.

(d) Make a comparative analysis of the two methods mentioned in the stem.

Seed and Input Cost Dynamics: The new method (SRI) requires significantly fewer resources than the conventional system, making it highly cost-effective. Water Management Paradigms: The two methodologies differ fundamentally in their irrigation needs. The traditional method relies on continuous flooding, which wastes massive amounts of water, increases pumping costs, and induces anaerobic soil conditions. SRI utilizes an Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD) schedule, reducing water consumption by 40%–50% while maintaining optimal soil moisture and aeration.


Tiller Growth and Development: Plant growth patterns vary greatly between the two systems due to spacing differences. In conventional fields, crowded planting limits growth, producing only 10–12 weak tillers per hill due to competition for nutrients and sunlight. In the SRI setup, wide spacing allows single young seedlings to grow without constraints, producing 30–50 robust tillers with exceptionally large, fully filled grain panicles.


Pest Susceptibility and Economic Conclusion: In conclusion, the structural differences also impact crop health and final profitability. The dense canopy of traditional farming traps humidity, making it highly vulnerable to devastating pest outbreaks and fungal infections that require expensive chemical treatments. SRI’s open spacing keeps humidity low, reducing pest pressure and chemical costs, which results in a higher net yield and a much more profitable harvest.

fullCQData.stem: Kafil's father had been cultivating groundnut and watermelon in a char area (river island) for a long time. After his father's death, desiring to cultivate field crops on his land, Kafil went to the agricultural office for advice. When the agricultural officer advised him regarding changing the soil texture, Kafil worked according to the advice and succeeded in cultivating field crops. The agricultural officer also told him, "Crops should be selected considering the soil texture."
knowledge_comprehension:
(a) What is the full form of SRI?

The full form of SRI is System of Rice Intensification.

(b) What effect does soil erosion have on soil?

Soil erosion severely degrades the soil by physically washing or blowing away the fertile topsoil layer, which contains the highest concentration of organic matter and essential plant nutrients.


As a result, the soil loses its water-holding capacity, microbial activity declines drastically, and the structural stability is ruined, ultimately reducing productive agricultural lands into barren, infertile zones with very poor crop yield potential.
full_cq:
(a) What is the full form of SRI?

The full form of SRI is System of Rice Intensification.

(b) What effect does soil erosion have on soil?

Soil erosion severely degrades the soil by physically washing or blowing away the fertile topsoil layer, which contains the highest concentration of organic matter and essential plant nutrients.


As a result, the soil loses its water-holding capacity, microbial activity declines drastically, and the structural stability is ruined, ultimately reducing productive agricultural lands into barren, infertile zones with very poor crop yield potential.

(c) Describe the advice that the agricultural officer gave to Kafil in light of the stem.

The agricultural officer advised Kafil on practical techniques to modify the physical behavior and structure of his sandy char land so he could successfully grow field crops.


Since char lands are dominantly sandy, they lack cohesion, have large pore spaces, and suffer from extremely low nutrient and water retention capacities, making them unsuitable for standard field crops like rice or wheat. To remedy this, the officer advised him to add massive amounts of organic manures, such as well-decomposed cow dung, compost, and green manure (Dhaincha), which bind the sand particles into stable aggregates.


Additionally, the officer advised him to practice soil mixing by carrying and incorporating heavy clay soil from nearby silted areas into his sandy plot to permanently alter its textural class toward a productive loam, alongside adopting frequent, light irrigation schedules to prevent deep percolation.

(d) Analyze the final statement of the stem.

Scientific Basis of the Statement: The agricultural officer's final statement, "Crops should be selected considering the soil texture," is a foundational principle of scientific agronomy. Soil texture—the relative proportion of sand, silt, and clay—is a permanent physical property that directly governs water availability, aeration, thermal properties, and nutrient dynamics, which dictate whether a crop can complete its lifecycle.


Crop Suitability based on Specific Textures: Different crops have evolved distinct root architectures and moisture requirements that align with specific textural classes. For instance, sandy soils with high aeration and loose resistance are ideal for taproots and tubers like groundnut, watermelon, and sweet potato, whereas heavy clay or loamy soils are essential for moisture-loving field crops like Boro rice and jute because they retain standing water and exchangeable nutrient ions over long periods.


Consequences of Mismatched Selection: If a farmer ignores soil texture and plants a crop blindly, it leads to absolute agricultural and financial failure. Planting rice in unmodified sand causes instant water loss and nutrient leaching, leaving the plant stunted, while planting root crops in heavy clay suffocates the root system due to waterlogging and compaction, leading to root rot and poor yields.


Conclusion: Therefore, Kafil's success in transitioning from watermelon to field crops completely validates this principle. By actively modifying the texture through heavy organic and clay inputs, he manually shifted the soil classification to match his desired field crops. Understanding and respecting soil textural limitations allows farmers to optimize resource use, pick the right crop varieties, and secure maximum yield sustainability.

(a) What is mulching?

Mulching is an agricultural practice where a layer of material—such as straw, plastic, or leaves—is applied over the surface of the soil to conserve moisture, improve soil health, and suppress weed growth.

(b) Why is Dhaincha suitable as a green manure?

Dhaincha (Sesbania) is highly suitable as a green manure because it is a rapid-growing leguminous crop that produces a substantial amount of organic biomass in a very short period.


Furthermore, its root systems harbor symbiotic Rhizobium bacteria that fix atmospheric nitrogen into the soil. Its soft, succulent green stems and leaves decompose rapidly when plowed into the ground, immediately enhancing the soil's organic matter content, nutrient availability, and overall physical structure.

(c) What kind of soil is marked as 'A' in the stem? Explain.

The soil marked as 'A' in the stem is Acidic Soil.


According to the standard chemical properties of soil, any soil that has a pH value below the neutral point of 7.0 is classified as acidic soil. Looking at the chart provided in the stem, 'A Soil' clearly spans across the lower values of the pH scale (ranging from 1 up to less than 7), indicating a high concentration of hydrogen ions ($H^+$) relative to hydroxyl ions ($OH^-$) in the soil solution.


Such acidic soils are typical in high-rainfall regions where basic cations like calcium and magnesium are leached away, leaving behind iron and aluminum compounds. This matches the structural classification taught in the textbook for soils with a pH under 7.0.

(d) "Soil marked as 'B' is suitable for the growth of all types of crops"—Give your opinion.

Context of the pH Range: The statement is highly accurate because the range marked as 'B' spans from pH 5.5 to 8.00, which represents the slightly acidic, neutral, and slightly alkaline zones. This specific range is considered the "ideal pH window" for general agriculture globally, as it avoids the toxic extremes of both high acidity and strong alkalinity.


Optimal Nutrient Availability: Within this 5.5 to 8.00 pH range, the biological and chemical availability of essential plant macro-nutrients (such as Nitrogen, Phosphorus, Potassium, Sulfur, Calcium, and Magnesium) reaches its maximum solubility. In contrast, if the pH drops below 5.5, phosphorus becomes locked up by iron and aluminum, while if it rises above 8.0, micro-nutrients like iron and manganese become completely unavailable to plants.


Thriving Microbial Activity: This pH range provides the most hospitable environment for beneficial soil microorganisms, including nitrogen-fixing bacteria (Rhizobium and Azotobacter) and decomposing fungi. These microbes break down organic materials into forms that crop roots can absorb, maintaining natural soil fertility and promoting healthy root development.


Conclusion: In conclusion, while certain specific plants have specialized preferences—such as tea preferring highly acidic soils and sugarbeet tolerating higher alkalinity—the vast majority of economic field crops, vegetables, and cereals thrive optimally within the 5.5 to 8.00 boundary. Therefore, the soil conditions marked as 'B' are fundamentally essential for maximizing overall agricultural diversity and crop productivity, completely justifying the statement.

(a) What is soil texture?

Soil texture is the relative proportion of various size groups of individual soil particles—namely sand, silt, and clay—present in a given soil mass.

(b) Why is it necessary to know soil pH? Explain.

Knowing soil pH is extremely necessary because it indicates the active acidity or alkalinity of the soil, which directly determines the availability of essential plant nutrients to the crop roots.


Furthermore, soil pH dictates the biological activity of beneficial soil microorganisms, such as nitrogen-fixing bacteria, which thrive best in a neutral pH range. If the pH is too high or too low, certain nutrients become locked and toxicities can occur; thus, knowing the pH helps farmers determine the correct soil amendments (like lime or sulfur) needed to maximize crop yield potential.

(c) Explain the technique indicated by the (?) mark in the stem.

The technique indicated by the question mark (?) in the stem is the preparation and application of Green Manure (সবুজ সার).


Green manure is an organic amendment technique where quick-growing, succulent leguminous crops—such as Dhaincha (Sesbania), Sunn hemp, or Cowpea—are cultivated directly on the field and plowed under into the soil using a tractor or power tiller while they are still in their green, pre-flowering vegetative stage.


Once incorporated, these green plants undergo rapid microbial decomposition under humid soil conditions, turning into rich humus that physically blends with the loose sandy particles shown in the stem, thereby initiating the conversion of coarse sandy soil into structural loam soil.

(d) Evaluate the role of the technique indicated by the (?) mark in increasing soil fertility.

Enrichment of Organic Biomass and Humus: The application of green manure (?) plays an indispensable role in uplifting soil fertility by adding substantial amounts of organic matter directly to the field. As the plowed-in green biomass decomposes, it breaks down into stable humus, which serves as a long-term reservoir for plant nutrients and improves the exchangeable nutrient-holding capacity of the soil layer.


Biological Nitrogen Fixation: Since green manure crops are predominantly from the leguminous family, their root nodes form a symbiotic relationship with Rhizobium bacteria to fix large amounts of atmospheric nitrogen into the soil matrix. When these plants are mixed into the sandy soil, they naturally release massive quantities of high-purity nitrogen, drastically minimizing the requirement for expensive, synthetic chemical fertilizers like urea.


Enhancement of Physical and Micro-biological Properties: Green manuring drastically improves the soil's physical environment by increasing porosity, which assists the sandy soil in transitioning into fertile loam soil with balanced water retention. Simultaneously, it serves as an energy-dense carbon source that fuels a massive population boom of beneficial soil microbes, accelerating nutrient cycling and rendering locked-up minerals fully soluble for crop uptake.


Conclusion: In conclusion, the inclusion of green manure alongside farmyard manure, compost, and vermicompost forms a comprehensive organic management framework. It acts as a primary catalyst for transforming degraded, low-fertility sandy soil into a highly productive loamy system, ensuring a sustained and optimal balance of fertility parameters essential for maximizing long-term crop yields.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Ripon has not been getting a good yield of crops on his land for several years. Applying an adequate amount of chemical fertilizer to the land did not yield any results. Regarding this problem, according to the advice of the agricultural officer, he tested the soil of his land at the "Soil Resource Development Institute" (SRDI). The test showed that the pH value of the soil on his land was 4.5. The agricultural officer explained the reasons for the creation of acidity in his land and gave necessary advice to solve the problem.
knowledge_comprehension:
(a) What is soil texture?

Soil texture is the relative proportion of various size groups of individual soil particles—namely sand, silt, and clay—present in a given soil mass.

(b) Why is organic matter called the "soul of soil"? Explain.

Organic matter is called the "soul of soil" because it fundamentally drives the biological, chemical, and physical life cycles that keep the soil active, fertile, and structured.


Without organic matter, the soil turns into a sterile, compacted mass of mineral particles unable to retain water or support crops. It serves as the primary food source for beneficial soil microorganisms, enhances water-holding capacity, improves soil aeration, and acts as a natural storehouse for all essential plant nutrients, making it indispensable for sustainable agriculture.
full_cq:
(a) What is soil texture?

Soil texture is the relative proportion of various size groups of individual soil particles—namely sand, silt, and clay—present in a given soil mass.

(b) Why is organic matter called the "soul of soil"? Explain.

Organic matter is called the "soul of soil" because it fundamentally drives the biological, chemical, and physical life cycles that keep the soil active, fertile, and structured.


Without organic matter, the soil turns into a sterile, compacted mass of mineral particles unable to retain water or support crops. It serves as the primary food source for beneficial soil microorganisms, enhances water-holding capacity, improves soil aeration, and acts as a natural storehouse for all essential plant nutrients, making it indispensable for sustainable agriculture.

(c) What could be the reasons for the problem on Mr. Ripon's land? Explain.

Based on the stem, Mr. Ripon's soil has a pH of 4.5, which classifies it as strongly acidic soil, and several key natural and management factors could be responsible for this critical problem.


Firstly, the excessive and continuous application of acid-forming chemical nitrogenous fertilizers (such as Ammonium Sulfate, Ammonium Phosphate, or Urea) without organic balancing changes the soil chemistry over time. When these fertilizers react with moisture, they release an abundance of hydrogen ions ($H^+$) into the soil solution, sharply driving down the pH level.


Secondly, natural environmental conditions such as heavy rainfall or excessive irrigation lead to severe leaching, where basic cations like Calcium ($Ca^{2+}$), Magnesium ($Mg^{2+}$), and Potassium ($K^+$) are washed away down into deeper layers. This leaves behind acidic iron and aluminum ions on the soil exchange complex, while the decomposition of local organic matter under anaerobic conditions can also accumulate organic acids, worsening the field's acidity.

(d) Evaluate the advice of the agricultural officer in solving Ripon's problem.

Identification of Soil State and Constraints: The agricultural officer's initiative to test the soil at the Soil Resource Development Institute (SRDI) was highly justified, as it successfully diagnosed the root cause of the crop failure—a highly toxic soil pH of 4.5. At this strongly acidic level, essential plant nutrients like phosphorus become chemically locked with aluminum and iron, rendering them unavailable, which explains why Mr. Ripon's heavy use of chemical fertilizers yielded no results.


Evaluation of Lime Treatment Advice: To neutralize this severe acidity and bring the pH back to an optimal range ($6.0 - 7.0$), the agricultural officer must have advised the application of agricultural lime—specifically Calcium Carbonate ($CaCO_3$) or Dolomite [$CaMg(CO_3)_2$]. Lime chemically neutralizes excess hydrogen ions, forces toxic aluminum out of the soil solution, and directly replenishes vital calcium and magnesium levels needed for cell wall development in crops.


Evaluation of Organic and Fertilizer Management: In addition to liming, the officer's advice would include a complete halt to acid-forming chemical fertilizers, replacing them with basic or neutral alternatives like Triple Super Phosphate (TSP) and calcium nitrate. Concurrently, the heavy incorporation of organic compost or green manure would be advised to act as a natural pH buffer, improving the soil's structure and locking away remaining toxic metallic elements through chelation.


Conclusion on Strategic Importance: In conclusion, the agricultural officer's comprehensive advice is perfectly aligned with HSC standard agronomic principles and is absolutely vital for restoring the land. By systematically addressing both the chemical imbalance through liming and the biological degradation through organic management, this scientific advice provides the only viable pathway to unlock bound nutrients, restore microbial activity, and ensure the return of high crop yields for Mr. Ripon.

fullCQData.stem: Goni Miah cultivates rice using conventional methods. But this year in Rasulpur village, he observes that the farmers have planted ten-day-old seedlings in a square pattern. They have planted one seedling per hill. He could not see any standing water on the land, but he noticed a few perforated pipes inserted vertically into the soil. Later, he learned about this matter in detail from the Sub-Assistant Agriculture Officer.
knowledge_comprehension:
(a) What is mulching?

Mulching is a soil management practice where a protective layer of organic or inorganic material (such as straw, grass, or plastic sheet) is spread over the soil surface around plants to retain moisture, control weeds, and maintain soil temperature.

(b) Explain the role of earthworms in increasing soil fertility.

Earthworms are considered natural soil conditioners and friends of farmers because they continuously burrow into the soil, feeding on organic matter. As they move through the soil profile, they swallow soil particles along with decaying organic debris, mix them inside their digestive tract, and excrete them as worm castings, which are highly enriched with essential plant nutrients like nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium.


Furthermore, the extensive network of burrows created by earthworms significantly enhances soil aeration, porosity, and water infiltration capacity. This loose, well-ventilated soil structure allows plant roots to penetrate deeper and absorb nutrients more efficiently, ultimately leading to a substantial improvement in overall soil fertility and crop productivity.

(c) Explain how the irrigation management system mentioned in the stem will help in profitable crop production.

The irrigation management system mentioned in the stem refers to the Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD) method, which is indicated by the perforated pipes inserted vertically into the soil. This is a highly efficient water-saving technology designed for rice cultivation that eliminates the need for continuous submergence of the field.


By monitoring the water table inside the perforated pipe, farmers only apply irrigation water when the water level drops to about 15 cm below the soil surface. This precise application prevents unnecessary water loss through evaporation, deep percolation, and runoff, thereby reducing the total number of irrigation cycles required throughout the crop cycle.


Consequently, this method significantly lowers fuel and electricity costs associated with operating irrigation pumps. By reducing production expenses while maintaining or even increasing crop yield, the AWD irrigation system serves as a highly profitable management strategy for modern farmers.

(d) Evaluate the logical justification of adopting the rice cultivation method observed by Goni Miah.

The rice cultivation method observed by Goni Miah is the System of Rice Intensification (SRI), characterized by transplanting young seedlings (around 10 days old), planting them singly in a square pattern, and keeping the soil moist rather than flooded. Adopting this innovative agricultural methodology is highly logical and justified for modern farming.


Firstly, the SRI method optimizes resource utilization while maximizing output. Transplanting a single young seedling per hill instead of multiple older seedlings reduces the seed requirement drastically, which lowers the initial cost of cultivation. The square spacing ensures that each plant receives ample sunlight, air, and soil nutrients, leading to robust root development and a higher number of effective tillers per hill.


Secondly, this method promotes environmental sustainability and soil health. Since the field is not continuously waterlogged, it discourages anaerobic conditions, thereby significantly reducing methane gas emissions—a potent greenhouse gas associated with traditional rice farming. The aerobic soil conditions also encourage beneficial microbial activity and healthier root growth, making the plants more resilient against pests, diseases, and lodging.


In conclusion, the SRI method breaks the conventional myth that rice must grow in standing water to yield well. By offering higher grain yields, substantial water savings, and lower production costs, the adoption of this method by Goni Miah is completely justified and economically viable for achieving food security and sustainable agricultural growth.

(c) Describe the methods for reclamation of the soil marked 'A' in the stem.

In the given stem, soil 'A' represents acidic soil because its pH range lies between 1 and 5. Reclaiming acidic soil is essential to make it suitable for optimal crop growth and to restore nutrient availability.


Application of Liming Materials: The primary method to neutralize soil acidity is the application of basic materials like agricultural limestone (calcium carbonate), dolomite, or slaked lime. These compounds react with the soil solution to increase the concentration of hydroxyl ions, effectively raising the pH level back toward a neutral range.


Organic Manure and Balanced Fertilization: Regularly adding generous amounts of well-decomposed organic matter, compost, or cow dung buffers the soil and reduces the toxic effects of aluminum and manganese. Additionally, using non-acidic fertilizers like Triple Super Phosphate (TSP) instead of highly acidifying chemical agents helps stabilize the soil structure.

(d) Evaluate the importance of reclaiming the soil marked 'A' and soil marked 'C' in the stem.

The reclamation of soil 'A' (acidic soil) and soil 'C' (alkaline soil) is of paramount importance for national food security, environmental sustainability, and optimizing agricultural output from vulnerable land resources.


Maximizing Nutrient Availability: In highly acidic soil 'A', essential plant nutrients like phosphorus, calcium, and magnesium become chemically locked, while toxic elements like aluminum and iron rise to harmful levels. Conversely, in alkaline soil 'C' (pH 9–14), micronutrients such as iron, zinc, manganese, and boron become completely unavailable to crops; reclaiming both soils ensures a balanced nutrient uptake environment.


Enhancing Microbial Activity and Crop Diversity: Extreme soil pH values drastically inhibit the growth and activity of beneficial soil microorganisms, including nitrogen-fixing bacteria and mycorrhizal fungi, which are vital for natural soil fertility. By adjusting the pH of both soils toward neutrality, farmers can diversify their crop selection from a few restricted tolerant varieties to a wide range of high-yielding cash crops and staples.


Economic and Agricultural Sustainability: Leaving these soils unamended leads to progressive land degradation, low fertilizer use efficiency, and substantial financial losses for farmers. Therefore, systematic reclamation using lime for soil 'A' and gypsum or sulfur for soil 'C' is highly critical to restore soil health, increase total arable land output, and secure sustainable farming systems.

Mulching is the agricultural practice of covering the soil surface around plants with organic materials (like straw, leaves) or synthetic sheets to conserve moisture, regulate temperature, and suppress weeds.

(b) Explain the role of green manure in modifying soil texture.

Green manure plays an important role in modifying the physical condition and structural arrangement of soil particles, although it cannot change the basic sand, silt, and clay proportions.


When green manure crops are plowed back into the soil, they decompose into humus, which binds loose sand particles together in sandy soils to improve water-holding capacity, and breaks up heavy clay aggregates in clayey soils to enhance aeration and water infiltration.

(c) Describe the characteristics of the soil marked as 'Ka' in the stem.

The soil marked as 'Ka' (ক) represents acidic soil because its pH value falls within the range of 0 to less than 7 on the soil pH scale.


Chemical and Nutrient Properties: In this acidic range, the solubility of toxic elements like aluminum, iron, and manganese increases significantly, which can harm plant roots. Conversely, the availability of primary essential plant nutrients such as phosphorus, calcium, magnesium, and molybdenum decreases drastically, leading to widespread nutrient deficiencies.


Microbiological Properties: Acidic conditions strongly inhibit the biological activities of beneficial soil microorganisms, especially nitrogen-fixing bacteria like Rhizobium and Azotobacter. Consequently, the natural decomposition of organic matter slows down, which negatively affects the overall fertility status and productivity of the soil.

(d) Analyze how the soil marked as 'Kha' can be made suitable for crop production.

The soil marked as 'Kha' (খ) represents alkaline soil since its pH value ranges from above 7 to 14. To make this soil highly productive and suitable for a wide range of agricultural crops, specific physical, chemical, and biological management practices must be systematically applied.


Chemical Amendments (Gypsum and Sulfur Application): The most effective way to lower the pH of alkaline soil is by adding chemical amendments such as gypsum (calcium sulfate) or elemental sulfur. Gypsum provides calcium ions that replace excess exchangeable sodium on soil particles, which can then be leached out of the root zone through proper drainage, while sulfur converts into sulfuric acid via microbial action to neutralize the alkalinity.


Organic Matter Enrichment: Applying generous amounts of organic manures, such as well-decomposed cow dung, compost, and green manure, helps lower soil pH. As these organic materials decompose, they release carbon dioxide and organic acids that naturally buffer the soil, neutralize alkaline compounds, and significantly improve the soil's structure and water retention capabilities.


Use of Acid-Forming Fertilizers and Selection of Tolerant Crops: Farmers should utilize acid-forming chemical fertilizers like Ammonium Sulfate or Ammonium Nitrate instead of basic ones to gradually reduce alkalinity. Furthermore, growing alkali-tolerant crops such as wheat, barley, sugar beet, and specific varieties of rice during the reclamation phase ensures economic returns while the soil health is being progressively restored.

fullCQData.stem: Safik went to visit his maternal uncle's house during the winter vacation. There he observed that most of the farmers cultivate rice using conventional methods. However, in the neighboring village, Safik noticed that the farmers had transplanted ten-day-old seedlings in a square pattern. They had planted one seedling per hill. He could not see any standing water on the land, but he noticed a few perforated pipes inserted vertically into the soil. Later, he learned about this matter in detail from the Upazila Agriculture Officer.
knowledge_comprehension:
(a) What is a biofungicide?

A biofungicide is a type of biological pest control agent derived from living organisms (such as beneficial bacteria, fungi, or plant extracts) that is used to control or suppress pathogenic fungal diseases in crops.

(b) Explain how the availability of plant nutrients increases through water irrigation.

Water irrigation acts as a crucial medium that directly enhances the availability and uptake of essential plant nutrients from the soil. Most plant nutrients exist in the soil in a solid or chemically bound state, which roots cannot absorb directly; irrigation water dissolves these essential minerals, forming a soil solution that allows plant roots to easily absorb nutrients through osmosis and transpiration pull.


Furthermore, adequate soil moisture facilitates the movement of nutrients towards the root zone via mass flow and diffusion. It also creates an optimal environment for beneficial soil microorganisms to decompose organic matter actively, releasing vital nutrients like nitrogen, phosphorus, and sulfur into accessible forms for the plants.

(a) What is a biofungicide?

A biofungicide is a type of biological pest control agent derived from living organisms (such as beneficial bacteria, fungi, or plant extracts) that is used to control or suppress pathogenic fungal diseases in crops.

(b) Explain how the availability of plant nutrients increases through water irrigation.

Water irrigation acts as a crucial medium that directly enhances the availability and uptake of essential plant nutrients from the soil. Most plant nutrients exist in the soil in a solid or chemically bound state, which roots cannot absorb directly; irrigation water dissolves these essential minerals, forming a soil solution that allows plant roots to easily absorb nutrients through osmosis and transpiration pull.


Furthermore, adequate soil moisture facilitates the movement of nutrients towards the root zone via mass flow and diffusion. It also creates an optimal environment for beneficial soil microorganisms to decompose organic matter actively, releasing vital nutrients like nitrogen, phosphorus, and sulfur into accessible forms for the plants.

(c) Describe the irrigation management system mentioned in the stem.

The irrigation management system described in the stem refers to the Alternate Wetting and Drying (AWD) method, which is identified by the vertically inserted perforated pipes in a field without standing water. This is an advanced, water-saving technology designed specifically for sustainable rice production.


Structure and Installation: In this method, a perforated plastic pipe (usually 30 cm long and 7–10 cm in diameter) is buried vertically into the soil so that 20 cm is below the surface and 10 cm remains above. The soil inside the pipe is removed so that the water level beneath the soil surface becomes clearly visible from the top.


Water Regulation Mechanism: Irrigation is not applied continuously; instead, the field is allowed to dry until the water level inside the pipe drops to 15 cm below the soil surface. Once it reaches this threshold, the field is irrigated again to a depth of about 5 cm, and this cyclical wetting and drying process is maintained until the flowering stage of the rice crop, significantly conserving water resources.

(d) Compare the cultivation method practiced at Safik's uncle's house with the method observed in the neighboring village.

The cultivation method practiced at Safik's uncle's house is the Traditional/Conventional Rice Cultivation Method, whereas the method observed in the neighboring village is the innovative System of Rice Intensification (SRI). A comparative analysis reveals distinct differences in resource efficiency and yield potential.


Seedling Age and Spacing: In the traditional method, older seedlings (30–40 days old) are haphazardly transplanted in large clusters (3–5 seedlings per hill) with irregular spacing. Conversely, the SRI method utilizes exceptionally young seedlings (only 10 days old) transplanted singly (one seedling per hill) in a precise square pattern (usually $25 \text{ cm} \times 25 \text{ cm}$), which dramatically reduces seed requirements and avoids root competition.


Water Management: Traditional rice farming relies heavily on continuous flooding, keeping the field submerged under standing water throughout the vegetative phase, which requires massive volumes of water. On the other hand, the SRI method combined with AWD keeps the soil moist but unsuffocated, intermittently allowing the field to dry out, which enhances root aeration and reduces water consumption by up to 50%.


Tillering and Crop Yield: Due to crowding and waterlogged conditions, traditional plants produce fewer effective tillers and have weak root systems, limiting overall yield. In contrast, the wider spacing and aerobic soil conditions of the SRI method promote extensive root systems and prolific tillering, leading to a significantly higher number of grains per panicle and a substantial boost in final crop productivity.

Conversely, the SRI method strictly utilizes young, vigorous seedlings that are only 8 to 12 days old, transplanted as a single seedling per hill to minimize transplant shock and encourage rapid adaptation.


Spacial Arrangement and Intercultural Operations: Conventional transplanting is usually done randomly with close, dense spacing, which restricts air circulation and complicates manual weeding. SRI requires a precise square matrix layout (typically $25 \text{ cm} \times 25 \text{ cm}$ or $30 \text{ cm} \times 30 \text{ cm}$), allowing ample room for root expansion, optimal sunlight interception, and the smooth operation of mechanical weeders which simultaneously aerate the soil.


Water Management and Environmental Impact: The traditional method demands continuous deep-water submergence of the field, creating anaerobic soil conditions that lead to weak root structures and high methane emissions. In contrast, SRI employs an alternate wetting and drying framework that keeps the soil consistently moist but never flooded, stimulating deeper root networks, maximizing nutrient absorption, and presenting an eco-friendly model for sustainable farming.

fullCQData.stem: Mr. Alam went to the agricultural office to test his land's soil for crop cultivation. In the laboratory, the agriculture officer prepared a soil solution and dipped a blue litmus paper into it. Due to this, the color of the litmus paper turned red. The agriculture officer said, "This condition of the soil can occur due to high rainfall or heavy irrigation and the application of certain fertilizers."
knowledge_comprehension:
(a) What is soil pH?

Soil pH is defined as the negative logarithm of the hydrogen ion concentration ($-\log[\text{H}^+]$) in the soil solution, which measures its degree of acidity or alkalinity.

(b) Explain the prospects of crop cultivation on Mr. Alam's land.

Based on the stem, when blue litmus paper is dipped into the soil solution of Mr. Alam's land, it turns red. This chemical reaction explicitly indicates that the soil of his land is acidic in nature (pH less than 7). The prospects of growing standard agricultural crops directly in such highly acidic soil conditions are generally poor.


Most major crops prefer a neutral to slightly acidic pH range (6.0 to 7.0) to flourish. In highly acidic soil, the availability of essential elements like nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium drops, while toxic minerals dissolve easily, stunting plant growth. However, if acid-tolerant crops like tea, pineapple, or jackfruit are selected, or if the soil is properly reclaimed using lime amendments, the cultivation prospects can become highly favorable.
full_cq:
(a) What is soil pH?

Soil pH is defined as the negative logarithm of the hydrogen ion concentration ($-\log[\text{H}^+]$) in the soil solution, which measures its degree of acidity or alkalinity.

(b) Explain the prospects of crop cultivation on Mr. Alam's land.

Based on the stem, when blue litmus paper is dipped into the soil solution of Mr. Alam's land, it turns red. This chemical reaction explicitly indicates that the soil of his land is acidic in nature (pH less than 7). The prospects of growing standard agricultural crops directly in such highly acidic soil conditions are generally poor.


Most major crops prefer a neutral to slightly acidic pH range (6.0 to 7.0) to flourish. In highly acidic soil, the availability of essential elements like nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium drops, while toxic minerals dissolve easily, stunting plant growth. However, if acid-tolerant crops like tea, pineapple, or jackfruit are selected, or if the soil is properly reclaimed using lime amendments, the cultivation prospects can become highly favorable.

(c) Describe the characteristics of Mr. Alam's land's soil.

The soil on Mr. Alam's land has been identified as acidic soil. According to the principles of agricultural soil science, this type of soil exhibits several distinctive physical, chemical, and biological properties.


Chemical and Nutrient Characteristics: Acidic soil contains a high concentration of hydrogen ions ($\text{H}^+$) and aluminum ions ($\text{Al}^{3+}$). In this environment, essential plant macro-nutrients—particularly phosphorus, calcium, magnesium, and boron—become chemically fixed and unavailable for plant uptake, while elements like iron, manganese, and aluminum reach toxic concentration levels.


Biological Characteristics: The high acidity severely suppresses the biological population and activity of beneficial soil microorganisms, such as Rhizobium and other nitrogen-fixing bacteria. Consequently, the natural rate of organic matter decomposition and nutrient recycling slows down drastically, leaving the soil structurally degraded and structurally compacted.

(d) Analyze the statement made by the agriculture officer.

The statement made by the agriculture officer is highly accurate and scientifically justified, as high rainfall, excessive irrigation, and the continuous application of specific chemical fertilizers are the primary drivers of soil acidification.


Impact of High Rainfall and Irrigation (Leaching Process): When a region experiences heavy rainfall or intense irrigation cycles, a massive volume of water percolates downward through the soil profile. This water washes away basic cations such as calcium ($\text{Ca}^{2+}$), magnesium ($\text{Mg}^{2+}$), and potassium ($\text{K}^+$) from the topsoil layer, leaving behind an excess of acidic hydrogen ($\text{H}^+$) and aluminum ions, which reduces the soil pH.


Impact of Specific Fertilizer Application: The long-term, intensive use of certain chemically acid-forming fertilizers—most notably nitrogenous fertilizers like Urea, Ammonium Sulfate, and Ammonium Nitrate—significantly contributes to acidity. When these fertilizers undergo nitrification in the soil, they release substantial amounts of hydrogen ions into the soil solution, directly increasing its acidity over time.


Conclusion: Therefore, the agriculture officer's analysis correctly identifies the core environmental and human-induced factors responsible for transforming Mr. Alam's land into acidic soil. Understanding these underlying causes is vital for executing corrective soil management strategies, such as adding lime to neutralize the acidity and adopting balanced organic fertilization.

3.chapterName: Chapter 3 : Special Production Related Agricultural Technology

mcqData.text: Ribbon retting is practiced in which crop?
mcqData.explanation: Ribbon retting is a water-saving method of extracting fiber from jute.

mcqData.text: At which stage should rogueing (removing unwanted plants) be done from seed crops? (i) Vegetative growth (ii) Flowering (iii) Maturity
mcqData.explanation: Rogueing must be done at all three stages to ensure seed purity.

mcqData.text: What is the process of pulling up the entire potato plant part above the soil called?
mcqData.explanation: Haulm pulling is done to stop tuber growth and thicken the skin before harvest.

mcqData.text: Why is the Queen Bee important in a bee colony?
mcqData.explanation: The Queen bee's only job is to lay eggs and ensure the growth of the colony.

mcqData.text: Which mushroom is cultivable during the summer season?
mcqData.explanation: Milky mushrooms prefer high temperatures ($30-35^{\circ}C$), making them ideal for summer.

mcqData.text: Stem: A heifer with 50% Friesian traits (from artificial insemination) is bred again with a 100% Friesian bull. Q: What % of Friesian traits will the second generation have?
mcqData.explanation: Calculation: $(50\% \text{ mother} + 100\% \text{ father}) / 2 = 75\%$.

mcqData.text: What is it called when the branches and tips of fruit trees gradually dry up and die from the top?
mcqData.explanation: Dieback is a common disease where drying starts from the tips move downwards.

mcqData.text: Which fertilizer has to be imported from abroad?
mcqData.explanation: Bangladesh is self-sufficient in Urea ($CO(NH_2)_2$) but imports most of its MOP ($KCl$) (Muriate of Potash).

mcqData.text: Mulching can be done using: (i) Water hyacinth (ii) Dry leaves (iii) Polythene
mcqData.explanation: Both organic and synthetic materials are effectively used for mulching.

mcqData.text: How many seedlings are planted per hill in the SRI method?
mcqData.explanation: SRI method strictly uses a single young seedling per hill.

mcqData.text: Which of the following is a legume crop?
mcqData.explanation: Sunn Hemp (Shonpat) is a leguminous plant that fixes nitrogen.

mcqData.text: Stem: Tarek Miah mixes Azolla (Khudipana) into his rice field soil, increasing yield and lowering costs. Q: Which bio-fertilizer is being added?
mcqData.explanation: Azolla works as a green manure/bio-fertilizer in rice fields.

mcqData.text: Stem: (Same as Q. 12) Q: Due to this bio-fertilizer, which chemical fertilizer is not required?
mcqData.explanation: Azolla fixes Nitrogen, so the need for Urea ($CO(NH_2)_2$) (Nitrogenous fertilizer) is reduced.

mcqData.text: When is the honey collection season in Bangladesh?
mcqData.explanation: Honey is mainly collected from late autumn through spring (October to May).

mcqData.text: Which pest attack is seen in mushrooms?
mcqData.explanation: Cockroaches and mites are common pests in mushroom cultivation beds.

mcqData.text: Curing is done in which of the following?
mcqData.explanation: Curing helps heal wounds on sweet potatoes and increases their shelf life.

mcqData.text: Which mushroom can be cultivated all year round?
mcqData.explanation: Oyster mushrooms are versatile and can be grown almost year-round in Bangladesh.

mcqData.text: Stem: Mr. Karim treated onion seeds with a chemical before sowing. Q: Which chemical is used for seed treatment?
mcqData.explanation: Vitavax-200 is a common fungicide used for seed treatment to prevent diseases.

mcqData.text: Stem: (Same as Q. 18) Q: Reasons for seed treatment: (i) Healthy seed production (ii) Protection from seed-borne diseases (iii) Increasing resistance
mcqData.explanation: Seed treatment ensures the plant starts healthy and resists early infections.

mcqData.text: What should be the ideal moisture content for proper seed storage?
mcqData.explanation: For long-term storage, seeds should be dried to 8-12% moisture.

mcqData.text: Stem: Farmer Ali Ahmed is worried about the price of Urea ($CO(NH_2)_2$). Agriculturist Mosharraf suggests using Azolla. Q: Which element is present in Azolla fertilizer?
mcqData.explanation: Azolla lives in symbiosis with Anabaena bacteria, which fixes atmospheric Nitrogen.

mcqData.text: Stem: (Same as Q. 21) Q: Why did the agriculturist suggest Azolla? Because it: (i) Has low production cost (ii) Can be grown anywhere (iii) Has no adverse effects
mcqData.explanation: Azolla requires specific water conditions to grow; it cannot literally grow "anywhere" without water.

mcqData.text: Isolation distance of seed crops can be maintained by: (i) Cultivating guard crops (ii) Adjusting sowing time (iii) Removing weeds frequently
mcqData.explanation: Sowing early/late (time isolation) and guard crops (physical isolation) prevent cross-pollination.

mcqData.text: What lives inside Azolla?
mcqData.explanation: Anabaena azollae is the blue-green algae that resides in Azolla leaves to fix nitrogen.

mcqData.text: Which shaped 'Ribboner' is used for Jute Ribbon Retting?
mcqData.explanation: Y-shaped wooden or bamboo ribboners are commonly used to strip jute bark.

mcqData.text: Queen Bee: (i) Largest in size (ii) Lays 1500 eggs daily (iii) Lives for 3 years
mcqData.explanation: A healthy queen bee is the largest in the colony and highly productive for about 3 years.

mcqData.text: Which agricultural research/corp. institution supplies seeds and fertilizers?
mcqData.explanation: BADC (Bangladesh Agricultural Development Corporation) is the primary supplier of inputs.

mcqData.text: What factors affect seed storage the most? (i) Temperature (ii) Moisture (iii) Sunlight
mcqData.explanation: Temperature and internal seed moisture are the critical factors for preserving seed life.

mcqData.text: What is the germination capacity of "good" seeds?
mcqData.explanation: High-quality agricultural seeds must have at least 70-80% germination capability.

mcqData.text: Math: Total seed sample weight is 20g. If other seeds and inert matter weigh 4g, what is the purity rate?
Which one is called 'Doctor's Fungus'?
mcqData.explanation: Trichoderma is called Doctor's Fungus because it kills harmful soil-borne pathogens.

mcqData.text: Which mushroom is most cultivated in BD considering nutrition and climate?
mcqData.explanation: Oyster mushrooms are popular due to their easy cultivation and climate suitability in BD.

mcqData.text: Which organization is an agricultural input supplier?
mcqData.explanation: BADC is responsible for the procurement and distribution of seeds and fertilizers.

mcqData.text: In how many weeks is Trichoderma-compost ready for use?
mcqData.explanation: Trichoderma accelerates decomposition, making compost ready in just 6-7 weeks.

mcqData.text: Which bio-fertilizer uses 'Peat Soil' as a carrier?
mcqData.explanation: Rhizobium inoculants are typically mixed with peat soil to keep the bacteria alive.

mcqData.text: Yield increases by placing bee boxes in fields of: (i) Potato (ii) Onion (iii) Mustard
mcqData.explanation: Bees are vital for cross-pollinated crops like onion and mustard; potato is mostly self-pollinated.

mcqData.text: Which breed of bull is primarily used for artificial insemination in BD?
mcqData.explanation: Holstein Friesian cross-breeding is the most common for improving milk production.

mcqData.text: At what % of pod maturity should mustard plants be harvested?
mcqData.explanation: Harvesting mustard when 80% of pods turn golden prevents seed shattering.

mcqData.text: What is known as 'Doctor's Fungus'?
mcqData.explanation: (Same as Q. 31) Trichoderma is a potent bio-control agent against plant diseases.

mcqData.text: What is the primary objective of artificial insemination?
mcqData.explanation: The core goal is to upgrade local breeds by using superior quality semen.

mcqData.text: What is the main purpose of maintaining isolation distance in seed crops?
mcqData.explanation: Isolation distance prevents cross-pollination between different varieties, keeping the breed pure.

mcqData.text: Stem: (Referring to Silk Moth/Caterpillar diagram) Q: What is the cultivation of the insect shown in the image called?
mcqData.explanation: The rearing of silkworms for the production of silk is called Sericulture.

mcqData.text: Stem: (Referring to Silk Moth diagram) Q: Through the rearing of the insect shown: (i) Silk is produced (ii) Foreign exchange is earned (iii) Self-employment is created
mcqData.explanation: Sericulture is a profitable agro-industry providing both raw silk and economic opportunities.

mcqData.text: What is the main objective of seed treatment?
mcqData.explanation: Treating seeds with fungicides kills pathogens on the seed coat, ensuring healthy germination.

mcqData.text: How many Queen bees are there in a single bee colony?
mcqData.explanation: A healthy bee colony always has only one fertile Queen bee.

mcqData.text: Which one is a bacterial bio-fertilizer?
mcqData.explanation: Rhizobium is a bacterium that forms nodules on legume roots; Azolla is an aquatic fern.

mcqData.text: Which bio-fertilizer acts as a bio-pesticide and treats the soil?
mcqData.explanation: Trichoderma is a fungus that suppresses soil-borne pathogens like Pythium and Fusarium.

mcqData.text: What is the main purpose of Rogueing?
mcqData.explanation: Rogueing involves removing "off-type" or unwanted plants from the seed field to ensure purity.

mcqData.text: What is bee-keeping called?
mcqData.explanation: Apiculture comes from the Latin 'Apis' (bee) and 'Cultura' (cultivation).

mcqData.text: What is the "seed" of a mushroom called?
mcqData.explanation: Mushrooms grow from mycelium called 'Spawn', which acts as their seed.

mcqData.text: Curing is performed on which crop?
mcqData.explanation: Curing (drying under shade) helps thicken the skin of potatoes and sweet potatoes for storage.

mcqData.text: The leaves of which tree are the main food for silkworms?
mcqData.explanation: Silk moths (Bombyx mori) feed exclusively on Mulberry (Tut) leaves.

mcqData.text: What is silk cultivation called?
mcqData.explanation: Sericulture is the specialized branch of agriculture dealing with silk production.

mcqData.text: What is Trichoderma?
mcqData.explanation: Trichoderma is a free-living, beneficial soil fungus used in composting and bio-control.

mcqData.text: Which of the following is a bacterium?
mcqData.explanation: Rhizobium is a nitrogen-fixing bacterium; Colletotrichum is a fungus.

mcqData.text: Stem: Shamim treats onion and mustard seeds and does intercultural operations. Q: Which of the following will Shamim use for seed treatment?
mcqData.explanation: Captan or Vitavax are standard fungicides used for treating seeds; others are insecticides.

mcqData.text: Stem: (Same as Q. 57) Q: The special care Shamim took includes: (i) Applying pesticide (ii) Maintaining isolation distance (iii) Timely rogueing
mcqData.explanation: For seed production, maintaining isolation and rogueing are the most critical "special" steps.

mcqData.text: What is added to the soil when leguminous crops are cultivated?
mcqData.explanation: Legumes have root nodules containing Rhizobium, which adds Nitrogen to the soil.

mcqData.text: Azolla is suitable for which crop?
mcqData.explanation: Azolla grows in standing water, making it a perfect nitrogen-fixing bio-fertilizer for rice.

mcqData.text: Which crop hosts the harmful Aphid (Jab poka) in seed production?
mcqData.explanation: Mustard is highly susceptible to Aphids, which can also spread to nearby seed crops.

mcqData.text: According to Bangladesh Seed Rules 1990, into how many classes are seeds divided?
mcqData.explanation: The 4 classes are: Breeder, Foundation, Certified, and Truthfully Labeled Seeds.

mcqData.text: (Duplicate of Q. 63) How many classes of seeds are in Seed Rules 1990?
mcqData.explanation: The four classes ensure a systematic chain of seed multiplication.

mcqData.text: (Duplicate of Q. 62) Which crop hosts harmful Aphids?
mcqData.explanation: Aphids (জাব পোকা) are the most destructive pests for mustard seed production.

mcqData.text: When is the ideal time for sowing onion seeds?
mcqData.explanation: Late autumn (Kartik-Agrahayan) is the peak time for sowing Rabi onions in BD.

mcqData.text: Which of the following is used for treating seed potatoes?
mcqData.explanation: A 3% Boric Acid solution is commonly used to treat seed potatoes against diseases.

mcqData.text: Which one is the primary (initial) seed?
mcqData.explanation: Breeder seed (প্রজনন বীজ) is the first stage, produced by plant breeders at research institutes.

mcqData.text: Which is the most important factor in crop cultivation?
mcqData.explanation: Seed is the fundamental unit; without a quality seed, other inputs cannot give a good yield.

mcqData.text: Why are seeds treated?
mcqData.explanation: Treatment targets fungi, bacteria, or insects attached to the seed.

mcqData.text: Which one is a seed treatment chemical?
mcqData.explanation: Ceresan is an old but well-known mercurial fungicide for seed treatment.

mcqData.text: Which of the following is a seed treatment medicine?
mcqData.explanation: While all are fungicides, Vitavax is specifically the most common for systemic seed treatment.

mcqData.text: At what percentage of leaf-top breakage should onions be harvested?
mcqData.explanation: When 60-70% of onion tops fall over/break, it indicates the bulbs are mature.

mcqData.text: What is the isolation distance for potato seed fields?
mcqData.explanation: A minimum distance of 30 meters is required to maintain the purity of seed potatoes.

mcqData.text: Aphid-hosting crops shouldn't be within how many meters of a seed potato field?
mcqData.explanation: A 60-meter buffer zone is recommended to keep aphids away from seed potatoes.

mcqData.text: How many 'eyes' should each cut piece of a seed potato have?
mcqData.explanation: Each cut piece must have at least 2 healthy eyes (buds) to ensure proper sprouting.

mcqData.text: Which is the major harmful pest of potato?
mcqData.explanation: Cutworms (কাটুই পোকা) are the most destructive as they cut the stems of young potato plants at night.

mcqData.text: How many days before harvesting should Haulm pulling be done?
mcqData.explanation: Haulm pulling 8-10 days before harvest helps the potato skin to mature and harden.

mcqData.text: As a result of Rogueing: (i) Varietal purity is maintained (ii) High-quality seeds are produced (iii) Germination capacity increases
mcqData.explanation: Rogueing ensures purity and quality but doesn't directly increase the biological germination capacity.

mcqData.text: Stem: Sohan's onion leaves turned silvery with small brown spots. Bulbs are becoming distorted. Q: What is the cause of this condition?
mcqData.explanation: Silvering of leaves is the classic symptom of Thrips (থ্রিপস) attack in onions.

mcqData.text: Stem: (Same as Q. 84) Q: What should be done to tackle this? (i) Use yellow traps (ii) Apply neem leaf extract (iii) Apply Bavistin
mcqData.explanation: Yellow sticky traps and organic extracts work for Thrips; Bavistin is a fungicide, not an insecticide.

mcqData.text: Stem: Farmer Hashem pulls up the potato plants with roots 8-10 days before harvest. Q: What is this action called?
mcqData.explanation: Removing the green parts (haulms) before harvest is known as Haulm pulling.

mcqData.text: Stem: (Same as Q. 86) Q: Results of this action: (i) Potato skin hardens (ii) No germ infection (iii) Injury-related wounds decrease
mcqData.explanation: A harder skin protects the potato from pathogens and physical damage during harvest and transport.

mcqData.text: Which of the following is used for seed treatment?
mcqData.explanation: Vitavax 200 is a systemic fungicide used to treat seeds; others are fertilizers.

mcqData.text: What is the germination capacity of good seeds?
mcqData.explanation: At least 70-80% germination is the standard for high-quality agricultural seeds.

mcqData.text: Which seed is NOT sold directly to farmers?
mcqData.explanation: Breeder seeds (প্রজনন বীজ) are kept at research stations for further multiplication.

mcqData.text: How is the varietal purity of a crop maintained?
mcqData.explanation: Rogueing is the specific process of removing off-types to keep the variety pure.

mcqData.text: What is the best method for maintaining varietal purity?
mcqData.explanation: (Same as Q. 91) Rogueing is indispensable for maintaining genetic purity in seed fields.

mcqData.text: Characteristics of good seeds:
mcqData.explanation: Good seeds should be uniform in size, shape, and color as per the specific variety.

mcqData.text: Rogueing results in the crop's:
mcqData.explanation: Its primary function is to eliminate genetic contamination.

mcqData.text: Where are seed potatoes stored in Bangladesh?
mcqData.explanation: Cold storage (হিমাগার) at $2-4^{\circ}C$ is essential for preserving seed potatoes long-term.

mcqData.text: What is the moisture level for collecting rice seeds?
mcqData.explanation: Rice seeds should be harvested and dried to reach a moisture level of 10-12%.

mcqData.text: Why is rogueing done in seed crop fields? (i) For varietal purity (ii) For seed purity (iii) For species conservation
mcqData.explanation: Rogueing maintains the purity of the specific variety and the seed lot.

mcqData.text: To protect seeds from pests biologically, which of the following is mixed? (i) Polygonum (ii) Vitavax (iii) Nishinda
mcqData.explanation: Polygonum (বিষকাটালী) and Nishinda are botanical/biological agents; Vitavax is chemical.

mcqData.text: Characteristics of good seeds: (i) Germination $>80\%$ (ii) Living embryo (iii) Moisture 12-15%
mcqData.explanation: Seed moisture should generally be lower (8-12%) for better storage life.

mcqData.text: Stem: Asad used pure seeds but found other plants/weeds in his field, which he removed in steps. Q: What does this indicate?
mcqData.explanation: Removing off-type plants and weeds from a seed crop field is called Rogueing.

mcqData.text: Stem: (Same as Q. 101) Q: Asad can remove unwanted plants: (i) Before flowering (ii) During flowering (iii) At maturity
mcqData.explanation: Rogueing is a continuous process that should be performed at all growth stages.

mcqData.text: What is applied to speed up the retting (rotting) of jute?
mcqData.explanation: Urea ($CO(NH_2)_2$) provides nitrogen to the bacteria, which accelerates the decomposition of jute bark.

mcqData.text: How many liters of water are needed to rot per kg of jute bark?
mcqData.explanation: For efficient retting of stripped bark (ribbons), 1.5 to 2.5 liters of water per kg is sufficient.

mcqData.text: What kind of substance is jute bark?
mcqData.explanation: Jute fibers are primarily composed of cellulose and lignin, hence called ligno-cellulose.

mcqData.text: Which institution discovered the Ribbon Retting method?
mcqData.explanation: BJRI (Bangladesh Jute Research Institute) developed this water-saving retting method.

mcqData.text: What is used to boost jute retting in the Ribbon Retting method?
mcqData.explanation: (Same as Q. 103) Urea ($CO(NH_2)_2$) is used as a catalyst for the bacteria involved in retting.

mcqData.text: What does the word 'Ribbon' mean?
mcqData.explanation: Ribbon' refers to the long, thin strips of jute bark stripped from the stalk.

mcqData.text: What is the device used for stripping jute bark called?
mcqData.explanation: A 'Ribboner' is the tool (often Y-shaped) used to strip the bark from green jute stalks.

mcqData.text: (Duplicate of Q. 104) Water required per kg of jute bark?
mcqData.explanation: This ratio is specific to the "Ribbon Retting" technique where water is scarce.

mcqData.text: (Duplicate of Q. 106) Inventor of Ribbon Retting?
mcqData.explanation: Bangladesh Jute Research Institute is the pioneer of this technology.

mcqData.text: How many days does it take to rot jute in the Ribbon Retting method?
mcqData.explanation: Ribbon retting is faster than traditional methods, taking usually 12-15 days.

mcqData.text: Reasons for Ribbon Retting: (i) Fiber quality improves (ii) Cuttings are reduced (iii) Requires less space
mcqData.explanation: It improves quality and saves space/water, but "cuttings" (barky ends) remain a separate issue.

mcqData.text: Advantages of Ribbon Retting: (i) Less time (ii) Less cost (iii) Fiber quality improves
mcqData.explanation: It is a highly efficient method for areas with water scarcity, improving overall fiber grade.

mcqData.text: Stem: Rofiq is worried due to lack of water for retting. The officer suggests a special method. Q: What is the name of this method?
mcqData.explanation: Ribbon retting is specifically designed for situations with extreme water scarcity.

mcqData.text: Stem: (Same as Q. 115) Q: In this retting method: (i) Fiber quality is good (ii) Jute stick stays strong (iii) Requires more space/water
mcqData.explanation: Since only bark is rotted, the sticks stay clean and strong; it requires less space and water.

mcqData.text: Stem: Rahim Miah has 115 kg of jute bark. How much Urea ($CO(NH_2)_2$) should he apply for retting?
mcqData.explanation: Calculation: 1 kg bark needs 10g Urea ($CO(NH_2)_2$). So, $115 \times 10 = 1150g = 1.15kg$. (Note: There is a typo in options, it should be 1.15 kg).

mcqData.text: Stem: (Same as Q. 117) Q: To get good fiber, Rahim will rot the bark in: (i) Marsh/Beel (ii) Large pots/Vats (iii) Soil pits
mcqData.explanation: For Ribbon Retting, large clay vats or polythene-lined pits are used for controlled rotting.

mcqData.text: Stem: Rofiq learns of a method to easily separate jute fiber. Q: What is the name of this method?
mcqData.explanation: The method of rotting stripped bark is universally known as Ribbon Retting.

mcqData.text: Stem: (Same as Q. 119) Q: In this method: (i) Needs more water (ii) Takes less time (iii) Labor cost is lower
mcqData.explanation: It takes less time and less water compared to traditional whole-stalk retting.

mcqData.text: Which of the following is called 'Doctor's Fungus'?
mcqData.explanation: Trichoderma is a beneficial fungus that destroys soil-borne harmful pathogens, acting as a biological doctor.

mcqData.text: Which bio-fertilizer treats the soil as a bio-pesticide?
mcqData.explanation: (Same as Q. 121) Trichoderma kills harmful fungi like Fusarium and Pythium in the soil.

mcqData.text: What is added to the soil when leguminous crops (bean family) are cultivated?
mcqData.explanation: Legumes harbor nitrogen-fixing bacteria in their root nodules, enriching the soil with nitrogen.

mcqData.text: Which of the following is Trichoderma?
mcqData.explanation: Trichoderma is a free-living, beneficial soil fungus that grows on organic matter.

mcqData.text: Which bacteria helps crops by fixing atmospheric nitrogen?
